diagrams.title: Merge Sort - Divide and Conquer Process
diagrams.description: A step-by-step visualization of Merge Sort showing the recursive splitting of an array into individual elements and the subsequent merging of sorted subarrays back together. The diagram displays an 8-element array [38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10] being processed through the divide phase (splitting into halves repeatedly) and the merge phase (combining sorted subarrays).
elements: Initial unsorted array with 7 elements in a horizontal row
Recursive splitting tree structure showing division into single elements
Merge operations with arrows showing comparison and combination
Sorted final array with comparison indicators during merge
Color-coded split levels (different colors for each recursion depth)
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates the divide-and-conquer strategy of Merge Sort, helping students understand how the algorithm breaks down the problem and rebuilds the sorted solution through merging sorted subarrays.

diagrams.title: Quick Sort - Partitioning Process
diagrams.description: A detailed visualization of the Quick Sort partitioning step showing how a pivot is selected, elements are rearranged around the pivot, and the array is partitioned into two subarrays. Uses the same input array [38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10] with pivot selection strategies visible (e.g., last element as pivot).
elements: Initial array with highlighted pivot element (last element)
Two pointers visualization (i and j) used in partitioning
Color coding: elements less than pivot (green), greater than pivot (red), pivot (yellow)
Arrow indicators showing element swaps during partitioning
Partition boundary line showing the final pivot position
Recursive call visualization for left and right subarrays
diagrams.purpose: Illustrates the partitioning mechanism which is the core of Quick Sort, showing how elements are rearranged relative to the pivot and how the divide step works without explicit merging.

diagrams.title: Merge Sort vs Quick Sort - Algorithm Comparison
diagrams.description: A side-by-side comparison diagram showing both algorithms processing the same input array [64, 34, 25, 12, 22, 11, 90]. Displays the step-by-step progression of both algorithms with their key characteristics: Merge Sort (divide and merge, O(n log n) always, requires extra space) vs Quick Sort (partition and recurse, O(n log n) average, in-place).
elements: Two parallel array visualizations starting with identical input
Merge Sort side: showing splitting tree and merge operations
Quick Sort side: showing pivot selections and partitions
Time complexity labels for best/average/worst cases
Space complexity indicators (in-place vs extra space needed)
Step counter or recursion depth indicators for both
Stability indicator (stable for Merge Sort, unstable for Quick Sort)
diagrams.purpose: Provides a comprehensive comparison to help students understand when to use each algorithm, highlighting the trade-offs between Merge Sort's guaranteed performance and Quick Sort's space efficiency.
